speaker: interviewer
text: [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We're back. OK, just

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me. This is June 21, 1995. We are with Mr. Harry Toporek in Lincolnwood, Illinois, USA. This is tape number three. Mr. Toporek, you were talking about the relationship between you and your brother and watching over each other. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Were there other incidents in Gutenbrunn that, that caused you to watch over your, your brother to the same extent, or he you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I always had to watch over him, because we're two different-- we're two brothers from the same parents. But we see things different. He will go organize things for every occasion is there. I will go organize wherever is a necessity. If I will risk my life, I will risk because if I am not going to do it, I will not survive anyhow. For him, wherever there is, was an opportunity, he went, he would like to organize. Don't get me wrong. Whatever he would have organized, I would have be a 50% partner to it. But you see, I had to watch over him. He would take things, risks that I would not. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Don't get me wrong. There isn't a thing he wouldn't do for me during the concentration camp years. And we had of wonderful relationship, not [? want. ?] But he-- I know that I had to watch him. And he always respect me. As a matter of fact, you know the numbers what we got, I got 145, 145, 183. He got 182. He-- I always had to keep an eye on him. Now, I'm just saying, you know, I wasn't worry about that he's going to get my number or I get his. I always had a responsibility to watch him, because he was very light-hearted to do things that I wouldn't do it. But he listened to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the two of you continued. What-- where did you go after Gutenbrunn?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were shipped from Gutenbrunn approximately either beginning of May, 1942, or the end of April. Because I was in Gutenbrunn, and I remember that was April the 20th, 1942. And we find out that the-- 1942, actually-- because we find out that the Warsaw ghetto uprising, that the Jewish people are getting wiped out out of Warsaw. And they put a resistance. So we did find this thing. That was in 1943. We were shipped later on-- the same year we were shipped on to Andrzejow. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In Andrzejow were there-- also were that kind of a similar camp like we had been Gutenbrunn and in Nekla. And we worked at the same railroad-- I mean, the same work on the railroads until September 1, 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to go back for a moment to date you mentioned, April 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now how is it that you remember that date so clearly?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was, that was my birthday. And that was actually what Himmler had in mind the 10th to give tickets for the Fuhrer a present, all the Jews from Warsaw. But he was unsuccessful. They were 1 back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you've come to a new camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Why do you think you were transported to another camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think at that time, there came out, there came a law from Berlin that all the Jewish camps should be liquidated. Because they liquidated our camp, all the camps around Poznan. All the Jewish camps were liquidated. And everything was shipped to-- we went to Birkenau. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember that was September 1, 1943. We were shipped to Birkenau. And I didn't even realize that that took us so long until I find a list last year with the numerical order. Then I find out that actually, we arrived according to numerical things, the numbers, that we arrived the 4th of September to Birkenau. So that took us actually four days to get there. But we did know that we're going to our doom.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you in Andrzejow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Andrzejow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Andrzejow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Andrzejow, I was from April-- end of April until September that year, 1943, September the 1st, as a matter of fact. This is also a day to remember, because the war broke out September the 1st.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now you're at Birkenau. Could you describe, describe the camp for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Birkenau. When we got into Birkenau, I thought that they're going in-- there were a place for tanneries out there. Now why did tanneries came in my mind? Because we used to burn part of the leather. And we could smell the same, the same smell-- skin, you know, like burning of skin. And I thought, I think that this will be a good place for us. You know, I thought to myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when they opened up the doors and they start screaming and hollering, and with whips, like [GERMAN], you know? Within five minute, you from normal people, they become animals. We didn't even know. They didn't-- we didn't care what's going to happen. They used to put us-- chased us out. And then they separate out the male from the female.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then this, we start making selections. I don't know if this was Mengele's. But I know that was a, an officer. He was going with a motorcycle, came in. And he asked-- we had to be undressed. No, no, we did not. We were not undressed, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came down. The male separate and the woman were separated. And he ask, how old are you? And what is your profession?

speaker: interviewee
text: Naturally, I told him that I am a tanner [INAUDIBLE]. And I'm 20 years old. That time, I was 20 years old. He said, he pointed to the side. So I had to go to the right. I know, because on the left, when they had the, the sick people. And I know that this goes in a different direction. And this goes on a different direction. I have seen one episode while the selection. One was called in that he should go to the left. And he try-- as to go to the left, he tried to run into the right. And the SS pushed him back with the guns. And they told him that [GERMAN]. You are dying with the heroic death. Honest to God, you see. I will remember this for the rest of my life. And they pushed him back that he should go to the left, [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was your daily routine of sorts in Birkenau? What would happen to you in the course of a day?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, we did not have any-- three days, we were without clothes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We used to push [INAUDIBLE] in barracks. For three days, we were only-- they had-- they chased us out. And we had to rub on the others' back to keep the bodies, the blood running. If not, they would freeze to death. Then, then the third day, they gave us clothing. When they gave us clothing, we didn't recognize each other. We didn't even recognize who is who, because they cut off the hair. And the gave us-- you know, a short guy got a long sweater or long jacket. You know, they didn't care. You got a jacket, slacks, and pair of shoes. I don't think that they shoes. No, we didn't have any shoes. They gave us no shoes at that time. And we were waiting. And every couple days, the SS used to come. We used to have to go twice-- every day, we had to go on the roll calls. And they used to make selections. Whoever is not fahig, they put them in right to the gas chamber. And then we have seen the four chimneys. The four chimneys were going in full swing. The sky was light-- was lit up red, not just at night. Even during the day, the sky was red. Four chimneys were constantly-- you could see the flames going above the chimneys maybe 15 to 20 feet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the planes, the American planes or the British planes, they didn't have to look far, I mean, to see where Auschwitz is. They could see the-- follow the flames. They have-- they could have find them very easily and destroy them. Looks like nobody was interested at that time. They had better business to do. Earlier, you talked about receiving a number. Was that in the form of a tattoo, or was that something you wore?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No, that was a tattoo. Each one of us, we had to go in in alphabetical order. And they gave us numbers. And it's a funny thing. I remove my number. And even to this day, they are four consecutive numbers alive. Three of them are in Chicago and one is in Israel. It's very unusual. From September 1943, this is a story for 52 year. And we're still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your number?

speaker: interviewee
text: 145183. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Then I became no more a human being. I became haftling and a haftling number, They didn't call me by name, nothing. Haftling number 145183, I got to be there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Birkenau, when people would-- the jobs that were given, were they always right in the camp itself? Or did people work outside the camp at any point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in Birkenau, actually, we were not doing any work on the outside. We were only in a quarantine in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We only did work, because they pushed us out. They chased us out from the barracks to take the jackets reverse, you know? And we put stones back and forth. Now we have seen in Birkenau also that transports came from Belgium, from Holland. Ghettos were wiped out. We have seen the, the way the women, the Jewish women, schlepped the little kids to the gas chamber. And the kids couldn't walk. And the mother was too weak to hold it. And the Germans with the dogs were beating them. You know, those are-- seeing that is--

speaker: interviewer
text: The children would see their--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's, it's, it's unreal. I mean, you know, nobody will-- can understand if you don't see it, and particularly when you got children of your own. Now at that time, lucky me that I was single. I didn't have any kids. I probably, if I would have my own children, that feeling. But you know, you could not have that feeling if you haven't got your own. And I-- you know, those, those scene, that is unforgettable scene. And then they used to take a barrack, whoever was incapable to work, they put them in a barrack naked. They didn't feed them at all. And when they had a chance to put them in crematorium, you see if a crematorium ever got open, you know, that-- for some reason or another, they didn't have to fulfill, they had a chance to get in, they, they took all those people in. They put them in the gas chamber. I'm talking about in Birkenau. I also remember women used to go to work at a distance that were far away. And they used to have children. We couldn't figure out. I'm sure that those children, they were not Jewish people. And they used to go to-- you know, and they used to-- they used to stay with the kids and play with them. It was far away. And one day, they took the-- they send everybody to work. And then they took the kids out. When the mothers came home, they find out that the children are not there. And they know what's happened to them. It was a terrible scene to see. You know, the mothers came home. I know that they were not Jewish mothers, because the Jewish children, they did not try to, to save them. The Jewish kids right-- went right away. But they took those, those kids. And they destroyed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- how did they destroy them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they went to the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you remember the mothers coming back, and their responses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the mothers came back from work. All of a sudden, you mean-- all of a sudden, we hear the screaming. You know, they had to send in-- they had to call in extra SS to quiet those people down with whips. And that was a terrible scene also to see. And we know. But we could not communicate with those people. We couldn't help them with it. I mean, those are things what I have seen in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any suicide attempts after that incident?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you said that you couldn't communicate with them, no one could comfort them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were separated. We were so separated. We only have seen them at a distance, you know? Don't forget that also that we've all wired in from-- in, in Birkenau was about 40 kilometers in square, square miles, camp in a camp in a camp. That was a [INAUDIBLE] camp, [? that mean-- ?] gypsy camp, and a Jewish camp, and a-- and that was so many camps in there. Even if you're going to run away, you're in another camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you talk about gypsies. And there were other nationalities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there were all kind of nationalities.

speaker: interviewer
text: What nationalities?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were a lot of Polish people. We were also mixed with the Polish people. There were people from France, Jews from all over the world. There were people that came that were that-- 1943, people came from Yugoslavia. At that time, I didn't even realize that those are Serbs, what the-- actually, they were shipped out, liquidate. They tried to liquidate them by the Croatian, because the Croatian collaborated with the Germans. And they shipped them out. They didn't give them any food whatsoever. They were worse treated than we. And they died, so many of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Serbs were treated worse?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Serbs, they put them in. They took people from Yugoslavia in a camp. They didn't give them no water, no bread, nothing. They didn't even count them. They didn't even chase them out. And all of a sudden within two, three days, they got three times as, as tall. They got skinny.

speaker: interviewer
text: And listen, we didn't-- we thought that Yugoslavia is Yugoslavia, you know? That-- in the meantime, we find out later that those were Serbs. And they came with the children. They came intact with their families. You could see fathers, mothers, children, grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: And within two, three weeks, they were all gone. They didn't took them-- they didn't even take them to the gas chamber. They just died for hunger.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were separated from, from you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were separated by wires.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you could not--

speaker: interviewee
text: They'd ask us for water. But we couldn't give them any, you see? There were guards. The guard was watching also.

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened next? What happened after Birkenau? In Birkenau, we have seen that the chimneys, no good ever will come out of it. It's only a matter of time, or it's going to be this month or next month that we-- we, we also, we find, we also know that they're taking transports to different camps. So we decided wherever is going to be an opportunity, we will try to register ourselves to go to work. So I went with my brother. And we registered, picked us up. They send us to a camp Jaworzno that was not too far away from Krakow and Auschwitz. There were coal mines.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they shipped us there. I don't know how many people were there-- were three truckloads. Probably about 300 people went to Jaworzno. Jaworzno was a concentration camp also that was part of Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Let's put it this way-- like a satellite, a satellite camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that camp compare with the others you had been to?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got in the death camp in Jaworzno, the first day-- that was probably the afternoon-- I looked at the vicinity around, you know, the-- see what goes on. And I tried to talk to people. And I've seen they got a cage. In that cage, they had rabbits, domesticated rabbits, what the lagerfuhrer usually used to have for themselves. And the skins, they used to dry out that shouldn't spoil-- shouldn't get spoiled.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I looked at this thing. I said, I-- for heaven sake. I mean, I could, I could manufacture this thing. I can know how to tan. Because in Lask, before I went in in the-- they shipped us in the camp, they organized a process of making skins what they need for the Russian front-- fur. So I registered myself. I start working there. So I learned what to do and how to do with those skins. When I have seen this thing, I decided that I'm going to go into the lagerfuhrer. And I will tell him that I can make a use out of those skins for the dry skins. I'm a specialist. And I can make good leather-- I mean good fur-- that you could have either for gloves, you could have for, for all kind of stuff. He says-- and he was really surprised to hear, I mean, that, you know, somebody could make a use out of it. And there were lots of them, maybe about five, six, or 10. He said, what about-- what will you need? What kind of chemicals will you need? I said I need flour, salt, what is available in the kitchen, and [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a stone that people used to use in Europe. After a shave, they used to use for the skin to make the skin smooth. They used to call it [INAUDIBLE]. It's a, it's a chemical. It's a stone, a crystal stone, like, you know, like-- it's clear. It's-- and just, they call it [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, nothing else? I said no. He said to me, come later on after the appell. And I will give you what you need. And I will give you the skins. So I took the skins, whatever he had. And I took them. But you can only do it after the work. And I will see to it that you should have-- get. So he brought me flour. So he asked me how much I need. I said I need a pound or something. You know, I didn't want to tell him too much. So he went. He took a, a a pot. He went to the kitchen. He brought me a pot of thing, of flour.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said I don't need so much. So he said, you need it. You will need it. What you don't need for the skin, you will need for yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he-- that's already a picnic. I mean, my brother already was boiling water. He know that I'm going to go. Because he told me to come after the appell, that he's going to give it to me. He gave me salt, flour-- and I didn't really need the flour. We could make it with flour and without flour. But [INAUDIBLE], you got to have, and salt. And he opened up an open door for me in the kitchen. I don't think that I would survive if the door wouldn't be opened by him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my brother was making already the water. And I was perspired. I had a cold drink. And I got pneumonia. And I couldn't go to work anymore. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I fainted. They took me in the hospital. After I was in the hospital for a couple days, I know my brother used to come. And I know that I got, I got pneumonia. I had high fever.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know it's a dangerous place. But there's nothing I can do. I mean, if I'm going to go to work, that's going to be the end of it. So whatever it's going to happen, going to happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was-- and I was in the hospital. My brother used to come every day to see me, you know, and to-- and I used to give him something. And he used to bring me something. We exchange words between us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after [INAUDIBLE], I see my brother doesn't come. He didn't come one day, the second day, three days. I didn't know what's happened. In the meantime, he did the same thing what I did. And he got sick. He went to work, though he worked for a kapo, Franz. He was a kapo. And he put them in the snow. He poured water over him in a winter day. That was probably in December. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They took him home. He couldn't walk. They took him home. They thought that he is dead. So they took him in a hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I asked my friend, David, what he is in Israel, what I just told you about him. We were together. He used to come to visit me. And he said-- through the window. He couldn't get in, in the, in the hospital-- through the window, he used to knock. And I opened the window. I said, where, where is Sam? He said, Sam is in the hospital. He didn't want to tell me he thought that he is dead. So I start looking for him. You can imagine after-- how he felt after being a day in the snow. He became so small. The first thing I, I, I asked him, where is your bread? He said he gave it away for water. That's the worst thing that you can do, drink water, you see? The water was not the healthiest thing. So I had to keep him-- to watch over him. I felt already better. My crisis was already over.

speaker: interviewee
text: And during the time, a transport-- a truckload of bread came for the kitchen. And they had an order to bring back a certain amount of people, mostly from the unfahig, or the unfit people, what cannot work. Naturally, the hospital is the first one. And they had to bring back a certain amount of people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was at that time in a hospital with my brother. And [INAUDIBLE] between them. And the lagerfuhrer was in front of the barrack from a hospital, in front of the door. And I went to him. And I told him who I am. He didn't recognize me, probably.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told him that I am the [GERMAN] what, what you gave me the flour and the thing. I just got sick. And I feel better already.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, I mean, do you think that you could leave me in here? I mean, I got so much of your stuff. He said, OK, you, you remain in here. So I was the only person in that block-- I mean, remained in the, in the, in the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my brother was shipped out. He was on the outside. He send me in. But I know that my brother is out. And I went out. And I ask him, please. We were six brothers and two sisters. Leave me my brother in here. And he said, you don't worry about your brother. Your brother, he goes in the convalescent home. Within a couple of weeks, he's going to be back. And he's going to be all right. I said, no. I know where he is going. If he's going to leave without me, I will never see him again. I know what they're doing over there with those people. He said, no, I could not leave your brother. But my brother have noted that I was talking to him. He know that I probably start negotiating about him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came in. And he looked at him. I said, where is your brother? He says, I show him. This is my brother. He said he want him to leave. I need so many more people. We had-- there weren't enough people in the hospital. I said, Herr Lagerfuhrer, if you're not going to leave my brother with me, I'm going to go with him. So he made the remark. I mean, not enough that you know where they're going, you still want to go with him? I said, yes, Herr Lagerfuhrer. And he said to the doctor-- the doctor is still alive. He is here in Chicago. He probably don't remember those thing. He said [GERMAN]. That's mean that they should give me a jacket with a pair of slacks. Because the rest of them didn't have anything, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: He gave me. And I put it on. And he said, no. Both brothers remain here, just like this. The lagerfuhrer was number one. He had the-- on, on the tattoo on his arm. He came-- he was a criminal. They brought him to control the camp, the haftling. They took all the, the biggest criminal to, to control them. This is the man will save my life and my brother's life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was so touched by it. I said, I want to go with him. And I didn't care any more. You know that comes to a, a a moment that you don't care in which direction you go. You want to get it over. That's it. And we remained. Then I find out that one more individual-- the doctor was hiding a friend of his. So actually, we remained three people. The rest were all gone. None of them came back.
